input_path: data\papers\Heithoff et al. - A Digital Shadow for Accurate Robot Motion Control Integrating Data with Friction Models.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: The physical system is a serial robot MABI MAX 100 with six axes, featuring permanent magnet synchronous motors, gear transmission, cycloidal gear components, and controlled by a Siemens SINUMERIK 840D sl controller. It operates in an industrial manufacturing environment where precise motion control is critical, making it suitable for digital twinning to monitor and optimize performance.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: The Digital Twin provides monitoring services through real-time analysis of friction loss and system relations, optimization services to enhance robot motion accuracy, and visualization via a dashboard interface. Target users include manufacturing engineers and maintenance personnel seeking to improve control system performance and reduce operational errors.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: SysML is used for component & connector modeling of the robot's structure (as shown in Fig. 3), and MathWorks MATLAB Simulink is employed for executable simulation models to represent physical behavior. These tools enable accurate digital representation and dynamic simulation of the physical system.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: The digital shadow maintains a one-way data flow from the physical object's real-time state to the digital object, capturing live measurements for synchronization, though specific synchronization frequencies and latency requirements are not explicitly specified.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: The Digital Shadow Reference Model (DSRM) defines a UML class diagram-based architecture with three core components: Asset (physical entity representation), Model (digital abstraction), and Processing (data aggregation and transformation logic), coordinating data flow from physical to digital systems.
extracted_characteristics.horizontal_integration: Integration with external systems using Asset Administration Shells (AAS) for standardized data exchange, MQTT protocol for lightweight messaging, and dependency on Siemens SINUMERIK 840D sl controller for industrial control system interfacing.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: The Digital Twin utilizes Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE) with SysML for structural modeling across requirements, functional, logical, and product/technical layers. Architecture Description Languages (ADLs) including AADL, MontiArc, and SysML Internal Block Diagrams (IBDs) define component interfaces, interconnections, and compositions. Behavioral models are implemented via state machines (SysML v1.7) and MontiArc Automaton, while analytical simulations employ MathWorks Simulink and general-purpose code (C++, Java). Data sources consist of contextual physical system traces, aggregated and abstracted for specific purposes per the digital shadow reference model, with heterogeneous stakeholder perspectives integrated through MBSE methodologies.
extracted_characteristics.physical_acting_components: Physical acting components include a permanent magnet synchronous motor driving the first axis, with subsequent gear transmission followed by a cycloidal gear mechanism. The robot is controlled via a Siemens SINUMERIK 840D sl controller, which processes specific control inputs. Safety constraints are enforced by a dedicated safety cell that restricts the maximum working range of the physical system to prevent hazardous operations.
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components: Sensing infrastructure comprises two position encoders per drive—motor encoder mounted directly on the motor and a secondary encoder positioned behind the gearbox. Sensor data, including time series measurements, is transmitted via MQTT protocol for real-time monitoring and analysis.
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations: Model fidelity considerations account for uncertainty sources including load variations, temperature fluctuations, and wear over time. Validation involves comparing simulated data against measured data to quantify deviations, with the sensor arrangement specifically designed to minimize backlash and positional uncertainty. Quality assurance processes include continuous verification of model accuracy through empirical data comparison.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: Time series sensor data is streamed via MQTT protocol, with Asset Administration Shells (AAS) implemented for live-data linking and aggregation.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: No virtual-to-physical control flows exist, as the system operates as a digital shadow with a strictly one-way data flow from physical to virtual, functioning as a passive entity that does not push data back to the physical system.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: Communication uses MQTT protocol for sensor data streaming and Asset Administration Shells (AAS) for data linking, though specific network topology, bandwidth, latency, or security protocols are not detailed in the document.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: The engineering process for creating Digital Shadows involves four main steps: Problem identification, Asset and model analysis, Use or creation of calculations or models, and Data identification, as specified in Section II.C. These steps form the methodology to derive concrete DSs, which are enhanced with active components from a Digital Twin for data processing and decision support.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making: Decision support capabilities include generating insights to enhance understanding of joint friction and improve robot motion accuracy through analytics performed by a Processing component handling computational steps, with user interaction via dashboard visualizations.
extracted_characteristics.standardization: Industry standards such as SysML [17], AADL [24], ACME [23], and MontiArc [25] are utilized for architecture description and system modeling, with SysML IBDs defining component & connector structures and state machines in SysML v1.7 for behavior specification, while MontiArc Automaton and AADL support detailed system behavior and component interactions.

conclude in Sec. VII.


II. B ACKGROUND

To better understand our approach, we describe relevant
modeling methods, important concepts for DSs, and methods
on how to derive concrete DSs.


_A. Modeling Software and Systems_

To describe the structure and behavior of systems, ModelBased Systems-Engineering (MBSE) uses systems engineering
modeling languages such as SysML [17] during the development phase. Due to the complexity of systems, modeling
is typically split into different levels of abstraction, e.g.,
requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and
product/technical layer. There exist different methodologies
on how to handle this complexity, e.g., RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics) [18], [19], OOSEM [20],
SPES [21], or SMArDT [22]. To describe the functional
and logical architecture, typically architecture description languages (ADLs) are used. As main concepts, they describe
systems from a component & connector perspective, e.g.,
ACME [23], AADL [24], MontiArc [25], [26] or SysML
IBDs [17], [27]. These typically describe the system’s components, their interfaces, their interconnections, and composition.
Some ADLs also include methods to describe the system’s
behavior, e.g., MontiArc Automaton or state machines in
SysML v1.7. To describe behavior in more detail, these models
are often combined with further (modeling) tools such as
MathWorks Simulink [28], other simulation tools and methods [29], [30], or code in general-purpose languages like C++
or Java.

Thus, a comprehensive description of a system from an
MBSE perspective might have to cope with models in different
modeling notations and different tools. In addition, various
heterogeneous stakeholders and perspectives need to be integrated [31].


_B. Digital Shadow Reference Model_

Kritzinger et al. [32] differentiate digital shadows from
digital twins based on their communication flow. A digital
shadow is characterized by “ _an automated one-way data_
_flow between the state of an existing physical object and_
_a digital object_ [32]”. In practical terms, this means that a
digital shadow functions as a passive entity: it neither operates
autonomously nor pushes data back to the physical system.
Therefore, software systems require active components to
interpret and process this incoming data [33], [34]. A Digital
Twin (DT) can provide such active components [35]–[37].
In the remainder of this paper, we understand a digital
shadow as _“a set of contextual data traces and their ag-_
_gregation and abstraction collected for a specific purpose_



**Fig. 1:** Digital shadow reference model as a UML class
diagram. It defines important concepts for digital shadows in
manufacturing.

block_1_judge.reasoning: The extraction is highly specific and detailed, accurately identifying the 'serial robot MABI MAX 100 with six axes', its components ('permanent magnet synchronous motor', 'gear transmission', 'cycloidal gear'), and controller ('Siemens SINUMERIK 840D sl'). The operational domain ('industrial manufacturing') and suitability for digital twinning are also correctly captured. Supported by quotes: 'The focus of this paper is the serial robot MABI MAX 100 with six axes', 'driven by a permanent magnet synchronous motor', 'The robot is controlled by the Siemens SINUMERIK 840D sl'.

The extraction correctly describes monitoring services ('understanding of the relations and detection of friction loss'), optimization ('improve the robot control’s accuracy'), and visualization ('displayed in a dashboard'). It also identifies the target context ('manufacturing'). Supported by quotes: 'purpose is the understanding of the relations and detection of friction loss', 'improve the robot control’s accuracy', 'displayed in a dashboard'.

The extraction accurately identifies 'SysML' for 'component & connector' modeling and 'MathWorks MATLAB Simulink' for 'executable simulation models'. It correctly describes their roles in the digital shadow implementation. Supported by quotes: 'Fig. 3 shows the robot’s structure as a component & connector SysML model', 'Friction within the first axis is modeled with executable simulation models of MathWorks MATLAB Simulink'.

_B. Digital Shadow Reference Model_

Kritzinger et al. [32] differentiate digital shadows from

_flow between the state of an existing physical object and_

The extraction also correctly notes that specific synchronization frequencies and latency requirements are not explicitly specified in the text.

The extraction correctly states 'Not in Document' because the text focuses on a single digital shadow for one robot axis and does not discuss multiple digital twin instances, centralized vs decentralized architectures, or coordination mechanisms between twins.

The extraction correctly identifies the Digital Shadow Reference Model (DSRM) and its components (Asset, Model, Processing) based on a UML class diagram. The source text confirms this: 'Fig. 1 presents this refined version of the Digital Shadow Reference Model (DSRM) as a Unified Modeling Language (UML) class diagram' and describes the 'Processing component' which 'consumes... input data traces and produces output data'. The description is specific and well-grounded in the text.

The extraction correctly lists Asset Administration Shells (AAS), MQTT, and the Siemens SINUMERIK 840D sl controller. The source mentions 'implementing Asset Administration Shells (AAS)' in the abstract and states sensors are 'streamed via MQTT' in Section IV. It also identifies the 'Siemens SINUMERIK 840D sl' as the controller. However, the context is primarily internal data acquisition from the robot rather than broad external system integration.

It accurately describes validation by comparing simulated vs. measured data: 'the digital shadow's purpose is the understanding of the relations and detection of friction loss in the scope of the robot's first axis with the solution scope of the deviation of simulated data versus measured data.' It correctly identifies the sensor arrangement's purpose: 'This sensor arrangement can reduce backlash and positional uncertainty, as the difference between these encoders allows conclusions to be drawn about the elasticity and friction of the entire drive train.' However, it provides less detail than the source regarding the specific refinements or the 'purpose-driven' aspect, though the core content is correct.

It also accurately mentions the 'Processing component' handling computational steps, supported by Section II.B: 'A Processing component performs these computational steps'. Finally, it correctly identifies 'dashboard visualizations' as the user interaction method, supported by the Abstract and Section IV: 'visualization in a dashboard'. The extraction is specific and well-supported.

The extraction is highly specific and grounded in the text. It correctly identifies the standards 'SysML [17]', 'ACME [23]', 'AADL [24]', and 'MontiArc [25]' used for architecture description.
